description:
Your role: You are a platform-engineer who is cleaning up temporary artefacts left behind by various CI/CD job runners.

    Current workspace (already present on disk):
      /home/user/ci_temp/
      ├── build1/
      │   ├── artifact1.tmp
      │   ├── keep.txt
      │   └── notes.tmp
      ├── build2/
      │   ├── debug.tmp
      │   ├── noise.tmp
      │   └── other.log
      └── build3/
          └── nested/
              ├── inner.log
              └── inner.tmp

    File contents that matter for this task:
      1. /home/user/ci_temp/build1/artifact1.tmp   …contains the literal substring “UNSAFE”
      2. /home/user/ci_temp/build2/debug.tmp       …contains the literal substring “UNSAFE”
      3. /home/user/ci_temp/build3/nested/inner.tmp …contains the literal substring “UNSAFE”
      4. The other “*.tmp” files do NOT contain that substring.

    Task you must accomplish:
      • Use a single, concise find … | xargs … pipeline (one command) to locate every “*.tmp” file *anywhere* under /home/user/ci_temp that contains the exact, case-sensitive string “UNSAFE”.
      • Produce a newline-separated list with the absolute path of every such file, sorted in ascending ASCII order.
      • Save that list to the file /home/user/ci_temp/unsafe_tmp_files.txt (create or overwrite as needed).

    Output/format requirements that the automated test will verify:
      1. A regular file must exist at the exact path /home/user/ci_temp/unsafe_tmp_files.txt.
      2. Its content must be exactly three lines long, no leading or trailing blank lines, no trailing spaces.
      3. The lines must be in strictly ascending ASCII order.
      4. Each line must be the absolute path to a “*.tmp” file that contains the substring “UNSAFE”.

      Correct final file content (for human reference, **do not print this literally in the terminal**):
        /home/user/ci_temp/build1/artifact1.tmp
        /home/user/ci_temp/build2/debug.tmp
        /home/user/ci_temp/build3/nested/inner.tmp

    Hints (not verified by the automated test):
      • Use “find … -type f -name '*.tmp' -print0” with “xargs -0 …” to avoid issues with spaces.
      • “grep -l” can be helpful to filter files that contain the target string before piping to sort.

    When you are done, you should only create/modify:
      • /home/user/ci_temp/unsafe_tmp_files.txt

    Nothing else in the filesystem should be changed.
truth:
Expected file: /home/user/ci_temp/unsafe_tmp_files.txt

    Expected exact contents (including EOL characters, LF-only):

    /home/user/ci_temp/build1/artifact1.tmp\n
    /home/user/ci_temp/build2/debug.tmp\n
    /home/user/ci_temp/build3/nested/inner.tmp\n
